questions.question: Write the complete CUDA kernel for vector addition with proper boundary checking and explain each line.
keyPoints: Define kernel with __global__ qualifier
Calculate global index using blockIdx, blockDim, threadIdx
Check boundary condition (i < n)
Perform addition c[i] = a[i] + b[i]
Explain stride pattern for handling large vectors

questions.question: Explain the host-device memory model in CUDA. Why can't we directly access device pointers from the host?
keyPoints: Separate physical memory spaces for host (CPU) and device (GPU)
Device pointers are invalid on host side
Need explicit cudaMemcpy for data transfer
cudaMalloc allocates in device memory space
Host cannot directly read/write GPU memory addresses

questions.question: Calculate the number of blocks and threads needed to add two vectors of size n=50,000 using a block size of 256 threads. Explain your answer.
keyPoints: Minimum threads needed = 50,000
Threads per block = 256
Blocks required = ceil(50000/256) = 196 blocks
Total threads launched = 196 × 256 = 50,176
Boundary check ensures only 50,000 elements processed

questions.question: What is memory coalescing? How does the vector addition kernel achieve coalesced memory access?
keyPoints: Coalesced access: consecutive threads access consecutive memory addresses
In vector addition, thread i accesses a[i] and b[i]
Threads in same warp read adjacent memory locations
Memory controller combines requests into fewer transactions
Improves bandwidth utilization significantly

questions.question: Explain the purpose of cudaDeviceSynchronize() in CUDA programs. When is it necessary?
keyPoints: Kernel launches are asynchronous - host continues without waiting
cudaDeviceSynchronize() forces host to wait for device completion
Necessary before reading results from device memory
Not always required - depends on code flow
Useful for timing and error checking

questions.question: If a CUDA kernel is launched with gridDim=1024 and blockDim=1024 for vector addition, but n=1000, how many threads actually perform computation?
keyPoints: Total threads launched = 1024 × 1024 = 1,048,576
Only 1000 elements exist in the vectors
Boundary condition (i < n) prevents out-of-bounds access
Exactly 1000 threads perform actual computation
Remaining threads idle after boundary check

questions.question: Write a complete CUDA program for vector addition that includes: (a) memory allocation on device, (b) data transfer from host to device, (c) kernel launch with appropriate grid/block configuration, (d) result transfer from device to host, and (e) proper memory deallocation. Also explain each step.
keyPoints: cudaMalloc for d_a, d_b, d_c allocation
cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyHostToDevice for inputs
Kernel launch syntax: add<<<blocks, threads>>>(d_a, d_b, d_c, n)
cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost for results
cudaFree for all three device pointers
Error handling and cudaDeviceSynchronize usage

questions.question: Derive the effective memory bandwidth formula for vector addition and calculate the expected time for adding two vectors of 1 million floats on a GPU with 400 GB/s memory bandwidth.
keyPoints: Total data movement = 3 × n × sizeof(float) bytes
For n=1M floats: 3 × 1M × 4 bytes = 12 MB = 12 × 10^-3 GB
Time = Data / Bandwidth = 0.012 GB / 400 GB/s = 3 × 10^-5 s = 30 microseconds
Practical efficiency ~80%, so actual time ~37.5 microseconds
Compare to CPU: GPU significantly faster for large vectors

questions.question: Explain the thread indexing mechanism in CUDA and derive the formula to map 2D thread indices to a 1D array index for vector addition. How would you modify the kernel to handle the case where vector length is not evenly divisible by block size?
keyPoints: Global index = blockIdx.x × blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
For non-uniform division, use boundary check: if (i < n)
Optional: stride pattern for vectors much larger than grid
Show complete kernel with boundary handling
Explain why boundary check prevents out-of-bounds access
